 {"id":"br-14bf2","title":"T10.2: Unit tests for ambient visual FX","description":"Test the ambient effect system:
- Health state determination logic (healthy/warning/critical/idle)
- Effect mapping (health state -> effect type + colors)
- AM_TUI_AMBIENT env var parsing (off/subtle/full)
- Transparency levels for each mode
- Effect disabled when AM_TUI_AMBIENT=off

Target: 8+ tests.","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance criteria:
- [ ] Test: healthy state maps to plasma with cool colors
- [ ] Test: warning state maps to plasma with warm colors
- [ ] Test: critical state maps to doom fire
- [ ] Test: idle state maps to metaballs
- [ ] Test: off mode produces no effect output
- [ ] Test: subtle mode applies 90% transparency
- [ ] Test: full mode applies 70% transparency
- [ ] Test: AM_TUI_AMBIENT parsing handles invalid values gracefully

Plan-space hardening additions:
- [ ] Comprehensive unit tests cover happy-path behavior, edge cases, invariants, and failure modes for this bead scope
- [ ] Integration/E2E (or PTY/stress where applicable) scripts validate end-user flows, degraded-mode behavior, and recovery paths for this bead
- [ ] Detailed diagnostics artifacts are produced (scenario IDs, timing breakdowns, failure reason codes, artifact paths, and repro commands)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"WildSpring","created_at":"2026-02-13T19:59:54.596028994Z","updated_at":"2026-02-19T02:17:20.901175447Z","closed_at":"2026-02-19T02:17:20.901153926Z","close_reason":"Ambient visual FX unit-test coverage completed (see comment #820).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["testing","tui","visual-fx"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-14bf2","depends_on_id":"br-2kc6j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-05T05:24:03Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-14bf2","depends_on_id":"br-3f3tq","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-05T05:24:03Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":61,"issue_id":"br-14bf2","author":"WildSpring","text":"Completed ambient visual FX unit-test coverage by combining existing ambient tests in tui_widgets.rs with new app-layer tests in tui_app.rs. Coverage now includes: health-state priority rules, effect mapping to Plasma/Plasma/DoomFire/Metaballs, AM_TUI_AMBIENT parse fallback, off/subtle/full opacity behavior, and app-level integration checks for off mode + health-input derivation (critical/error, idle, ring-utilization warning). Validation: rch exec -- cargo check -p mcp-agent-mail-server --all-targets passed (tests compile). Direct test execution on current rch workers fails at link stage due missing system sqlite3 library (-lsqlite3).","created_at":"2026-02-19T02:17:17Z"}]}
 {"id":"br-14tc9","title":"T4.1: Generate dynamic Mermaid syntax from agent contact and message data","description":"Create functions that generate Mermaid diagram syntax from live Agent Mail data.

AGENT COMMUNICATION GRAPH:
```mermaid
graph LR
    A[GoldHawk] -->|42 msgs| B[SilverFox]
    A -->|18 msgs| C[CoralBadger]
    B -->|7 msgs| C
    style A fill:#accent
    style B fill:#secondary
```

THREAD FLOW:
```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant GoldHawk
    participant SilverFox
    GoldHawk->>SilverFox: [br-123] Start implementation
    SilverFox->>GoldHawk: Re: [br-123] Progress update
```

Functions:
- `generate_contact_graph_mermaid(contacts, messages) -> String`
- `generate_thread_flow_mermaid(thread_messages) -> String`
- `generate_system_overview_mermaid(projects, agents, reservations) -> String`

FILES: tui_widgets.rs (new mermaid generation section)","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance criteria:
- [ ] Contact graph Mermaid syntax generated correctly
- [ ] Thread flow sequence diagram generated correctly
- [ ] Edge labels include message counts
- [ ] Node styles use agent-specific colors
- [ ] Generated syntax validates against Mermaid grammar
- [ ] Unit tests with sample data

Plan-space hardening additions:
- [ ] Integration/E2E (or PTY/stress where applicable) scripts validate end-user flows, degraded-mode behavior, and recovery paths for this bead
- [ ] Test and benchmark outputs include detailed structured diagnostics (scenario IDs, timings, error/reason codes, artifact paths, and repro commands)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"SilverOtter","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:07:31.109806210Z","updated_at":"2026-02-15T04:16:20.034707265Z","closed_at":"2026-02-15T04:16:20.034685143Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["data","mermaid","tui"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-14tc9","depends_on_id":"br-38uii","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-05T05:24:03Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":62,"issue_id":"br-14tc9","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Note from br-241rg prerequisite pass: ftui-extras does not expose a separate  feature flag. Use feature  (it gates mermaid modules in ftui-extras/src/lib.rs). Workspace Cargo.toml now enables .","created_at":"2026-02-13T20:07:20Z"},{"id":63,"issue_id":"br-14tc9","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Correction (supersedes previous malformed comment): ftui-extras does not expose a separate mermaid feature flag. Use feature name diagram (this gates mermaid modules in ftui-extras/src/lib.rs). Workspace Cargo.toml in mcp_agent_mail_rust now enables diagram.","created_at":"2026-02-13T20:07:30Z"},{"id":64,"issue_id":"br-14tc9","author":"SilverOtter","text":"Implemented Mermaid syntax generation in crates/mcp-agent-mail-server/src/tui_widgets.rs via new section + helpers. Added: generate_contact_graph_mermaid(contacts, messages), generate_thread_flow_mermaid(thread_messages), generate_system_overview_mermaid(projects, agents, reservations). Added supporting structs: MermaidThreadMessage, MermaidProjectNode, MermaidAgentNode, MermaidReservationNode. Added deterministic agent color styling (stable hash -> palette), message-count edge labels, deterministic ordering, and label sanitization for Mermaid-safe output. Added unit coverage with Mermaid parser validation (ftui_extras::mermaid::parse) and diagnostics test emitting scenario/timing. New tests: mermaid_contact_graph_counts_styles_and_parses; mermaid_thread_flow_builds_sequence_and_parses; mermaid_system_overview_links_projects_agents_and_reservations; mermaid_system_overview_handles_duplicate_agent_names_across_projects; mermaid_generators_emit_parseable_diagnostics. Validation via rch: cargo check -p mcp-agent-mail-server --lib PASS; cargo test -p mcp-agent-mail-server mermaid_ -- --nocapture PASS (5 tests).","created_at":"2026-02-15T04:16:17Z"}]}
 {"id":"br-154k","title":"T2.3: Implement multi-seed flake detection (N re-runs with different seeds, parallel)","description":"## Objective
Add deterministic multi-seed flake detection to distinguish nondeterministic failures from stable failures.

## Work
- Implement rerun orchestration across configurable seed sets and run counts.
- Support safe parallel execution while preserving deterministic result aggregation.
- Classify outcomes into stable-fail, flaky, and inconclusive buckets with clear evidence.

## Deliverable
A native flake detector that provides reproducible, high-confidence flakiness signals.","acceptance_criteria":"## Acceptance Criteria
- Implementation is native Rust and cross-platform, introducing no new operational dependency on external shell/Python tooling.
- Preserves required legacy behavior (or documented intentional changes) with deterministic CLI/API semantics and stable `--json` output where applicable.
- Emits structured diagnostics and runtime telemetry sufficient for user-facing troubleshooting and CI artifact capture.
- Has comprehensive test coverage tied to this behavior (unit + integration + e2e where relevant), including negative-path assertions.
- Demonstrates reproducible behavior under CI constraints (timeouts, retries, deterministic ordering/seed controls).
- Produces explicit logging output suitable for rapid root-cause analysis when failures occur.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:24:32.145033710Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-12T04:48:50.536523869Z","closed_at":"2026-02-12T04:48:50.536502700Z","close_reason":"Implemented by RubyPrairie: extend_seeds(), run_multi_seed_subprocess(), MultiSeedConfig with 5 tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-154k","depends_on_id":"br-1z66","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-05T05:24:03Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":65,"issue_id":"br-154k","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# T2.3: Implement Multi-Seed Flake Detection

## What to build
Run a specific test N times with different seeds to determine if it's a true flake
(intermittent failure) vs a hard failure (always fails). Replaces the detect mode
in flake_triage.sh.

## Current bash behavior (lines ~100-140 of flake_triage.sh)
```bash
for seed in $(seq 1 $num_seeds); do
    if cargo test "$test_name" -- --seed "$seed" 2>/dev/null; then
        passes=$((passes + 1))
    else
        fails=$((fails + 1))
    fi
done
```

## Rust implementation
```rust
struct FlakeDetectionConfig {
    test_name: String,
    num_seeds: u32,        // default: 10
    parallel: bool,        // run seeds concurrently
    timeout_per_run: Duration,  // default: 60s
    cargo_args: Vec<String>,    // extra args to pass to cargo test
}

struct FlakeDetectionResult {
    test_name: String,
    total_runs: u32,
    passes: u32,
    failures: u32,
    is_flaky: bool,         // true if 0 < failures < total_runs
    flake_rate: f64,        // failures / total_runs
    failing_seeds: Vec<u64>,
    passing_seeds: Vec<u64>,
    elapsed: Duration,
}

fn detect_flake(config: &FlakeDetectionConfig) -> Result<FlakeDetectionResult, Error>
```

## Implementation notes
- Use std::process::Command to run `cargo test <test_name>` with `--seed <N>` if supported,
  or with env var `TEST_SEED=<N>` as an alternative
- For --parallel mode, use std::thread::scope to run seeds concurrently
  (limit concurrency to num_cpus to avoid oversubscription)
- Capture exit code only (not full output) for speed
- Timeout: kill the subprocess if it exceeds timeout_per_run

## Location
crates/mcp-agent-mail-cli/src/flake_triage.rs
","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:29:38Z"},{"id":66,"issue_id":"br-154k","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## CORRECTION from deep audit

The exact cargo test invocation in multi-seed mode targets THREE packages:
  HARNESS_SEED="$seed" cargo test -p mcp-agent-mail-core -p mcp-agent-mail-server -p mcp-agent-mail-db "$test_name" -- --nocapture >/dev/null 2>&1

The DEFAULT_FLAKE_SEEDS (17 values) are ALREADY defined in core::flake_triage (lines 346-368):
0, 1, 2, 42, 100, 255, 1000, 12345, 65535, 999_999, 0xDEAD_BEEF, 0xCAFE_BABE, 0x1234_5678, 0xFFFF_FFFF, u64::MAX, u64::MAX/2, u64::MAX/3

When N > 17, the bash extends with $RANDOM$RANDOM. Rust should extend with thread_rng().

The run_with_seeds() function already exists in core::flake_triage. The CLI just needs to:
1. Create a closure that spawns `cargo test` with HARNESS_SEED env var
2. Pass it to run_with_seeds()
3. Format the resulting FlakeReport

Per-seed output should be colorized (like bash): green PASS / red FAIL with seed value.
","created_at":"2026-02-12T01:49:01Z"}]}
+{"id":"br-1591x","title":"Classify SQLite lock errors as RESOURCE_BUSY across query/tool surfaces","description":"Observed while onboarding in /data/projects/mcp_agent_mail_rust on 2026-03-06/07: register_agent via MCP tools returned generic DATABASE_ERROR, and repo-local 'am robot' flows failed with database-is-locked symptoms. In the Rust code, crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/src/queries.rs maps every SqlError to DbError::Sqlite, while crates/mcp-agent-mail-tools/src/lib.rs only emits RESOURCE_BUSY for DbError::ResourceBusy. This loses actionable lock/busy semantics and makes active contention look like an opaque transient DB failure. Objective: detect lock/busy signatures at the DB boundary (or equivalent central mapping), preserve corruption/pool semantics, and add regression coverage proving lock-class errors surface as RESOURCE_BUSY to tool callers.","notes":"Implemented DB/MCP lock-error reclassification patch. Remote verification: mcp-agent-mail-tools targeted test passes; db no-default-features verification exposed separate existing feature-gating compile issues unrelated to this patch; live MCP Agent Mail registration still blocked by generic database error on server write path.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-03-07T03:38:49.599178003Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-07T04:00:38.140677595Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":1162,"issue_id":"br-1591x","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Traced the failure path: raw SQLite lock/busy conditions are flattened to DbError::Sqlite in the DB layer, then surfaced as generic DATABASE_ERROR in tool responses. Added a fix locally to classify lock-like SQL errors as ResourceBusy and to map lock-like Sqlite/Pool/Schema messages to RESOURCE_BUSY at the MCP layer. Agent-mail registration is still blocked against the live server while verification is running via rch.","created_at":"2026-03-07T03:44:35Z"},{"id":1163,"issue_id":"br-1591x","author":"Codex","text":"Diagnosis: SQLite lock/busy errors from db writes were being flattened into generic DATABASE_ERROR at the tool boundary. Patched db query error mapping to classify lock-like SqlError messages as ResourceBusy, and added a second guard in tool error translation so lock-like Sqlite/Pool/Schema messages also return RESOURCE_BUSY. Verification is running remotely via rch.","created_at":"2026-03-07T03:49:24Z"},{"id":1164,"issue_id":"br-1591x","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Verification update: isolated rch run passed for mcp-agent-mail-tools unit test db_error_to_mcp_error_maps_sqlite_lock_as_resource_busy. Live MCP Agent Mail diagnostics currently show health=red, pool_utilization_pct=100, pool_total_connections=1, and register_agent errors at 19/19, which explains why session bootstrap and introductions are blocked independently of the repo patch.","created_at":"2026-03-07T04:00:38Z"}]}
 {"id":"br-15dv","title":"[epic] Extreme Performance & Resilience: 1000+ Concurrent Agents","description":"## Goal
Make **MCP Agent Mail (Rust)** operationally boring at extreme concurrency: **1000+ agents** performing reads+writes simultaneously without deadlocks, data loss, unbounded memory growth, or IO collapse.

This project is already a feature-parity Rust port of the legacy Python server. This epic is explicitly about going beyond parity: hard performance budgets, reliability engineering, and overload-safe architecture.

## Why This Exists (Failure Modes From Python Era)
The original Python implementation degraded badly under high concurrency due to:
- SQLite contention and SQLITE_BUSY storms during bursts.
- Git index.lock contention and expensive synchronous git operations on hot paths.
- Unbounded in-memory queues and log/event amplification under load.
- Cascading IO failures (many tiny writes, lock thrash, background tasks competing with the hot path).

## Non-Negotiables
- Correctness: No lost messages/acks/reservations/contacts. DB remains the source of truth.
- Bounded resources: Every queue has a capacity and explicit policy when full.
- Graceful degradation: Under overload, shed non-critical work first and return actionable errors (429/503 with retry guidance); never cliff-drop into slow synchronous IO.
- Deterministic validation: Every optimization must preserve conformance fixtures/golden outputs (isomorphism proof) unless we intentionally change the contract and update fixtures.

## Target Load Model (Working Assumption)
- Agents: 1000 registered agents across 1-50 projects.
- Concurrency: 100-500 concurrent HTTP requests (thundering herd patterns included).
- Mix: read-heavy (fetch_inbox/resources/search) with periodic write bursts (send_message, reservations, acks).
- Pathologies: message storms (1000 msgs in <1s), inbox polling storms, many reservations, large payload attempts, worker failure, disk pressure.

## Strategy (Extreme Optimization Loop)
1. Baseline representative scenarios (p50/p95/p99 latency, throughput, RSS growth, queue depths).
2. Instrument the true bottlenecks (DB pool waits, SQLite busy, WBQ/commit backlog, lock contention).
3. Change one lever at a time (Opportunity Matrix score >= 2.0), with a rollback plan.
4. Prove behavior unchanged (conformance + golden outputs + invariants).
5. Repeat: bottlenecks shift.

## Consistency Model (Intentional)
- SQLite DB is authoritative for queryable state (messages, recipients, reservations, contacts, acks).
- Git archive is an async audit log, best-effort and overload-safe. It must never block tool responses.
- Signals/notifications are correctness-adjacent (UX responsiveness) and must be explicitly categorized as required vs skippable under overload.

## Tracks
Work is organized into tracks under this epic: db, storage/git, concurrency/backpressure, memory, algorithms, resilience, build+bench, observability, stress/chaos.","acceptance_criteria":"Acceptance Criteria:
- 1000-agent simulation (defined in br-15dv.9.*) runs for 10+ minutes with:
  - 0 panics
  - 0 deadlocks/hangs
  - 0 data-loss (DB invariants hold)
- Under sustained overload, system enters Yellow/Red modes and sheds non-critical work without cascading failures.
- All queues are bounded; overflow policies are explicit and tested.
- p95/p99 latency budgets exist per scenario and are enforced in CI (scaled-down on PR, full on nightly/adhoc).
- cargo test, cargo clippy -D warnings, cargo fmt --check pass.
- Crash/restart test: kill server mid-burst, restart, PRAGMA integrity_check passes and system resumes without manual intervention.
- Memory plateau: after warmup, RSS growth < 5% over 30 minutes in soak scenario.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:24:55.516413906Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T19:24:52.480637279Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T19:24:52.480611280Z","close_reason":"All 12 tracks closed: DB optimization (1), storage/git (2), concurrency (3), memory (4), algorithms (5), resilience (6), build/bench (7), observability (8), stress tests (9), SLOs (10), profiling (11), architecture docs (12). Epic complete.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["perf","resilience","scale"],"comments":[{"id":67,"issue_id":"br-15dv","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"ARCHITECTURE CONTEXT: The Python mcp_agent_mail used SQLAlchemy (async) + GitPython with no connection pooling, no write batching, and blocking git operations on every message send. It regularly failed at 10+ concurrent agents due to: (1) SQLite 'database is locked' errors from too many writers, (2) git index.lock storms when multiple agents committed simultaneously, (3) memory leaks from unclosed DB sessions, (4) cascading timeouts when one slow git operation blocked the entire system. The Rust port already improves on this with: WAL mode, connection pooling, write-behind queue, and commit coalescing. But the current defaults (7 connections, 256 WBQ, 100 commit queue) are sized for the Python-era workload of ~10 agents. Scaling to 1000+ requires a fundamentally different approach: adaptive sizing, lock elimination, spatial indexing, and system-wide backpressure.","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:34:38Z"}]}
 {"id":"br-15dv.1","title":"[track] Database layer optimization for 1000+ agent concurrency","description":"The SQLite database layer is the #1 bottleneck for scaling beyond ~10 concurrent agents. Current defaults: 3+4=7 connection pool, 1000-entry cache cap, 60s agent TTL, single global mutex for deferred touches, single global mutex for query tracking. At 1000 agents with 10% active (100 concurrent tool calls), the pool is 14x oversubscribed, the cache thrashes constantly, and mutex contention serializes all requests. This track addresses: pool sizing, cache overhaul, lock sharding, query tracker redesign, missing indexes, PRAGMA tuning, config caching, prepared statements, and write batching.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:25:36.069380158Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T09:29:12.609431193Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T09:29:12.609348158Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["db","perf","scale"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-15dv.1","depends_on_id":"br-15dv","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-05T05:24:03Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-15dv.1.1","title":"Adaptive connection pool sizing with read/write separation","description":"PROBLEM: Pool defaults to 3+4=7 connections. At 100 concurrent tool calls (10% of 1000 agents), each connection handles 14+ requests → 60s acquire timeouts fire constantly.

CURRENT CODE (pool.rs:24-27):
  DEFAULT_POOL_SIZE = 3
  DEFAULT_MAX_OVERFLOW = 4
  DEFAULT_POOL_TIMEOUT_MS = 60_000

SOLUTION: Implement adaptive pool sizing that scales with observed load:
1. Increase defaults to min=20, max=100 (SQLite WAL handles concurrent readers well)
2. Add adaptive sizing: monitor acquire latency, grow pool when p95 > 50ms, shrink when utilization < 30%
3. Separate read pool (large, many connections) from write pool (small, serialized via WAL)
4. Add pool warmup on startup (pre-open min connections)
5. Add per-pool metrics: active, idle, waiting, acquire_latency_p95

VALIDATION: stress_pool_exhaustion_recovery test must pass with pool_size=20, 200 concurrent threads.

FILES: crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/src/pool.rs, crates/mcp-agent-mail-core/src/config.rs","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"RubyPrairie","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:00.722082725Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-07T21:15:25.455163400Z","closed_at":"2026-02-07T21:15:25.455139855Z","close_reason":"Implemented adaptive pool sizing: defaults 25+75=100 (up from 3+4=7), auto_pool_size() heuristic based on CPU count, 15s acquire timeout (down from 60s). All tests passing.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["db","perf","pool"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-15dv.1.1","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-05T05:24:03Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-15dv.1.1","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.1.10","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-05T05:24:03Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-15dv.1.1","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.8.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-05T05:24:03Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":68,"issue_id":"br-15dv.1.1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"SIZING RATIONALE: SQLite WAL mode allows unlimited concurrent readers but serializes writers. With 1000 agents, read:write ratio is approximately 3:1 (fetch_inbox, search, resources are reads; send_message, ack, reserve are writes). Optimal config: 50 read connections (instant, no contention), 5 write connections (serialized but fast with WAL). Total: 55 connections. This matches PostgreSQL best practices of poolSize = (2 * cores) + spindles, adapted for SQLite's simpler model. The adaptive sizing adds a feedback loop: if acquire_latency_p95 > 50ms, add 10% more connections up to max_connections. If utilization < 30% for 5 minutes, shrink by 10% down to min_connections.","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:34:38Z"}]}
